value.comparison_basis: daily moves measured against Monday 2026-06-22 where source pages give previous-session changes
value.dominant_dynamic: risk-off rotation from a U.S. chip and AI valuation selloff, alongside a stronger dollar, lower Treasury yields, higher volatility, and continued oil-risk-premium compression

text: June 23, 2026 was a normal Tuesday macro session. The official Treasury CSV's newest row was dated 06/23/2026, so Treasury moves are measured against 06/22/2026. Across the verified market pages, the day combined a U.S. chip and AI valuation selloff, a stronger dollar, lower Treasury yields, higher equity volatility, and further crude weakness tied to US-Iran supply-normalization headlines.

calculation.method: Read official Treasury par-yield CSV; one-day changes equal 06/23/2026 row minus 06/22/2026 row; 2s10s equals 10Y minus 2Y in basis points.

text: U.S. Treasury par yields fell on June 23, led by the front end: 2Y dropped 8 bp to 4.16%, while 10Y slipped 1 bp to 4.50% and 30Y slipped 1 bp to 4.94%. The 2s10s spread widened from 27 bp to 34 bp, a front-end-led bull steepening consistent with safe-haven demand and some near-term policy-risk relief during the equity selloff.

value.narrative_level: around 101.3, highest since April last year
drivers: prospect of Fed rate hikes this year
flight to safety triggered by technology-stock selling
stronger-than-expected flash S&P Global PMI data
attention to the week's PCE inflation report

text: The U.S. Dollar Index rose to 101.3749 on June 23, up 0.35% on the session and 2.15% over the prior month. Trading Economics described the move as a fourth consecutive gain to about 101.3, the highest since April last year, supported by Fed-hike expectations, a flight to safety from technology-stock selling, stronger-than-expected flash S&P Global PMI data, and focus on the week's PCE inflation report.

value.level_note: both lowest in nearly three months per Trading Economics narrative
value.driver: US-Iran progress and a new 60-day U.S. waiver permitting global buyers, including American refineries, to purchase Iranian crude and fuels
caveats: Tehran disputed U.S. claims on immediate return of nuclear inspectors
long-term stability depends on negotiations, Lebanon, and the Strait of Hormuz

text: Crude continued to shed risk premium on June 23. Brent fell to $76.77/bbl, down 1.45% on the day and 20.58% over the month, while WTI fell to $72.95/bbl, down 1.23% on the day and 22.30% over the month. Trading Economics tied the declines to US-Iran progress and a new 60-day U.S. waiver allowing global buyers, including American refineries, to buy Iranian crude and fuels, while noting unresolved caveats around nuclear inspections, Lebanon, and the Strait of Hormuz.

value.context: volatility rose during the U.S. equity and technology-stock selloff

calculation.method: Trading Economics stated USVIX 19.49 and increase of 2.21 since the previous trading session; implied prior = 19.49 - 2.21 = 17.28.

text: Equity volatility moved higher: Trading Economics showed USVIX at 19.49 on June 23, up 2.21 points from the previous trading session, implying a prior level of 17.28. The rise fits the same risk-off rotation visible in U.S. equities, the dollar, and Treasuries.

value.driver: selloff in heavyweight chip producers and skepticism that AI infrastructure spending by hyperscalers will meet returns
value.offset: healthcare and consumer defensive gains aided a near-flat Dow

text: The risk-asset trigger was a U.S. chip and AI valuation unwind. Trading Economics put the US500 at 7,367 on June 23, down 1.42% from the previous session, and described U.S. stock indices as negative, with the S&P 500 down 1.4% and the Nasdaq 100 down 3.3% as heavyweight chip producers sold off on skepticism that AI infrastructure spending by hyperscalers will meet returns. Healthcare and consumer defensive gains helped keep the Dow near flat, but the macro footprint was risk-off.
